title: Templates
text: The templates/ directory contains Jupyter notebooks for common workflows.

title: Templates
text:
Available Templates

fitting.ipynb - Model fitting workflow
parameter_shifting.ipynb - Parameter sensitivity analysis
context.ipynb - Context component exploration
linear_memory.ipynb - Memory component exploration

title: Templates
text:
Using Templates

Copy a template to your working directory
Modify parameters and data paths
Run the notebook

cp templates/fitting.ipynb my_fitting_analysis.ipynb
jupyter notebook my_fitting_analysis.ipynb
Coming soon: detailed template documentation

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Note



For the full workflow, see the parameter shifting template notebook.

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Overview
Understanding how CMR parameters affect model predictions is crucial for:

Interpreting fits: What does a high drift rate mean behaviorally?
Setting bounds: What parameter ranges produce realistic behavior?
Identifying parameters: Can different parameters produce similar effects?

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Basic Parameter Sweep
Vary one parameter while holding others at baseline:
from jax import random
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jaxcmr.models.cmr import CMR
from jaxcmr.simulation import simulate_study_and_free_recall
from jaxcmr.analyses.spc import spc
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Baseline parameters
base_params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 1.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.6,
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

# Parameter values to test
drift_rates = [0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8]

# Simulate for each value
list_length = 10
n_trials = 500
rng = random.PRNGKey(0)

results = {}
for drift in drift_rates:
    params = base_params.copy()
    params["encoding_drift_rate"] = drift

    all_recalls = []
    for i in range(n_trials):
        rng, rng_iter = random.split(rng)
        model = CMR(list_length, params)
        present = jnp.arange(1, list_length + 1)
        model, recalls = simulate_study_and_free_recall(model, present, rng_iter)
        all_recalls.append(recalls)

    results[drift] = jnp.stack(all_recalls)

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Visualizing Effects
Plot how the serial position curve changes:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

for drift, recalls in results.items():
    # Compute SPC
    curve = spc(recalls, list_length)
    ax.plot(range(1, list_length + 1), curve, label=f"drift={drift}")

ax.set_xlabel("Serial Position")
ax.set_ylabel("Recall Probability")
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Effect of Encoding Drift Rate on SPC")
plt.show()

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Common Parameter Effects

Encoding Drift Rate



Value
Effect




Low (~0.2)
Weak recency, more uniform recall


High (~0.8)
Strong recency, temporal contiguity





Primacy Parameters



Change
Effect




Higher primacy_scale
Stronger primacy (better early item recall)


Higher primacy_decay
Primacy effect drops off faster





Choice Sensitivity



Value
Effect




Low (~0.2)
More random recall order


High (~2.0)
More deterministic (most active item wins)





Stop Parameters







Change
Effect




Higher stop_probability_scale
Shorter recall sequences


Higher stop_probability_growth
Stopping increases faster with output position

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Parameter Interactions
Some parameters interact—changing one may require adjusting another:

Drift rate + learning rate: Both affect association strength
Shared support + item support: Both affect baseline retrieval
Primacy scale + decay: Trade off in shaping the primacy curve

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Practical Tips

Start with published values: Use parameters from CMR papers as baselines
Vary one at a time: Understand each parameter before varying multiple
Check behavioral measures: Use SPC, CRP, and PNR to assess effects
Consider identifiability: Some parameter combinations may produce identical behavior

title: Parameter Sensitivity
text:
Next Steps

Fitting - Apply these insights to parameter estimation
Analyses - Full set of behavioral measures

title: Workflow
text: This section goes beyond the API to cover research workflow and tooling.

title: Workflow
text:
The Modeling Cycle
Computational modeling research typically follows this cycle:

Identify a research question - What behavioral phenomenon needs explanation?
Identify a mechanism - What cognitive process could produce this behavior?
Implement the mechanism - Add it to a model as a new component or parameter
Validate the mechanism - Use parameter sensitivity analysis to verify it behaves as expected
Fit to data - Optimize parameters to maximize fit
Evaluate - Does the new mechanism improve fit over baseline?
Iterate or publish - Refine or share findings

title: Workflow
text:
Sections

Parameter Sensitivity - Understanding parameter effects
Templates - Using the templates/ directory
Papermill - Batch notebook execution
Research Tools - Other utilities for research

title: Evaluation
text: This section covers how to evaluate models against data.

title: Evaluation
text:
Overview
Model evaluation in jaxcmr involves three interconnected processes:

Simulation - Generate predictions from a model
Fitting - Find parameters that best explain observed data
Comparison - Compare models to determine which best accounts for the data

title: Evaluation
text:
Sections

Fitting - Parameter optimization with differential evolution
Simulation - Generating model predictions
Comparison - Formal model comparison

title: Model Comparison
text: After fitting multiple models, you need to determine which best accounts for the data.

title: Model Comparison
text:
Approaches

Likelihood comparison - Compare log-likelihoods directly
Information criteria - AIC, BIC for model selection
Cross-validation - Out-of-sample prediction accuracy

Coming soon: detailed comparison methods

title: Models
text: This section documents the memory search models available in jaxcmr.

title: Models
text:
Why Do We Model Memory?
Free recall experiments reveal robust patterns:

Recency: Items from the end of a list are recalled first and most often
Primacy: Items from the beginning are also recalled well
Contiguity: Recalled items tend to come from nearby serial positions

CMR (Context Maintenance and Retrieval) explains these patterns through a simple mechanism: temporal context that drifts during study and is reinstated during retrieval.

title: Models
text:
Model Components
Models are composed of three core components:

Context - Temporal context representations
Memory - Associative memory (MFC, MCF)
Termination - When to stop recalling

title: Models
text:
Base Model

CMR - The Context Maintenance and Retrieval model
Parameters - Complete parameter reference

title: Models
text:
Creating Custom Models

Custom Models - Implementing your own variants

title: Termination
text: Termination policies determine when a model stops retrieving items.

title: Termination
Termination
text:
Available Implementations

title: Context
text: Context is a slowly drifting representation that tracks temporal position during study and retrieval.

title: Context
text:
The Context Protocol
Any context implementation must satisfy the Context protocol:
class Context(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    initial_state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    size: int

    def integrate(
        self,
        context_input: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"],
        drift_rate: Float_,
    ) -> "Context":
        """Returns context after integrating input representation."""
        ...

title: Distance CRP
text:
Note



See templates/distcrp.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Distance CRP
text:
Overview
Distance CRP shows transition probabilities as a function of semantic distance between items, revealing semantic clustering effects.

title: Distance CRP
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.distcrp import plot_dist_crp

plot_dist_crp(
    datasets=[data],
    features=semantic_embeddings,
    labels=["Data"],
)
Requires semantic feature vectors (e.g., word embeddings) for items.

title: Subject Relative Accuracy Curve
text:
Note



See templates/srac.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Subject Relative Accuracy Curve
text:
Overview
The Subject Relative Accuracy Curve (SRAC) normalizes recall probability relative to each subject’s overall performance, revealing the shape of serial position effects independent of overall memory strength.

title: Subject Relative Accuracy Curve
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.srac import srac, plot_srac

result = srac(data["recalls"], list_length=16)
plot_srac(datasets=[data], labels=["Data"])

title: Probability of Nth Recall
text:
Note



See templates/pnr.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Probability of Nth Recall
text:
Overview
Probability of Nth Recall (PNR) shows which study positions are most likely to be recalled at each output position (1st recall, 2nd recall, etc.).
This reveals:

Where recall typically starts (usually recency)
How recall transitions across the list
Differences in early vs. late output positions

title: Probability of Nth Recall
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.pnr import pnr, plot_pnr

# Compute PNR
result = pnr(data["recalls"], list_length=16)

# Plot
plot_pnr(
    datasets=[data],
    labels=["Data"],
)

title: Probability of Nth Recall
text:
Interpretation



Pattern
Interpretation




First recall from end
Strong recency initiation


Progression toward primacy
Temporal contiguity + primacy


Random across positions
Weak temporal structure

text:
Note



See templates/repcrp.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Repetition CRP
text:
Overview
Repetition CRP analyzes transition probabilities for experiments where items are repeated during study, revealing how repetition affects temporal contiguity.

title: Repetition CRP
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.repcrp import plot_rep_crp

plot_rep_crp(datasets=[data], labels=["Data"])

title: Behavioral Analyses
text: jaxcmr provides functions for computing standard behavioral measures from free recall experiments. Each analysis can be applied to both empirical data and model simulations.

title: Behavioral Analyses
text:
Getting Started

Importing Data - Loading recall datasets
Scoring Data - Computing basic metrics
Custom Analyses - Implementing your own

title: Behavioral Analyses
text:
Analysis Categories

Serial Position
How recall probability varies with study position.



Analysis
Description




SPC
Serial Position Curve - recall probability by position


PNR
Probability of Nth Recall - what position is recalled first, second, etc.


SRAC
Subject Relative Accuracy Curve





Temporal Contiguity
How recall transitions relate to study order.



Analysis
Description




CRP
Conditional Response Probability - transition probabilities by lag


DistCRP
Distance-based CRP - transitions by semantic distance





Category Effects
Effects of item categories on recall.



Analysis
Description




CatCRP
Category CRP - within vs. between category transitions


CatSPC
Category SPC - serial position by category





Repetition Effects
Analyses for experiments with repeated items.



Analysis
Description




RepCRP
Repetition CRP - transitions involving repeated items


Compound Cueing
Effects of compound cues on retrieval





Error Analyses
Patterns of recall errors.



Analysis
Description




Intrusions
Extra-list intrusion rates


Omissions
Items not recalled

title: Behavioral Analyses
text:
Using Analyses
All analysis functions follow a similar pattern:
from jaxcmr.analyses.spc import spc, plot_spc

# Compute analysis
result = spc(data, list_length, trial_mask=None)

# Or use the plotting function
plot_spc(
    datasets=[data, sim],
    trial_masks=[mask, mask],
    labels=["Data", "Model"],
)

title: Behavioral Analyses
text:
Comparing Model and Data
Most plotting functions accept multiple datasets for comparison:
from jaxcmr.analyses.crp import plot_crp

plot_crp(
    datasets=[simulation, empirical_data],
    trial_masks=[sim_mask, data_mask],
    labels=["CMR Model", "Observed"],
)

title: Behavioral Analyses
text:
Available in Templates
Each analysis has a corresponding template notebook in templates/:

templates/spc.ipynb
templates/crp.ipynb
templates/pnr.ipynb
etc.

These templates show complete workflows for computing and visualizing each measure.

title: Omission Errors
text:
Note



See templates/omission_error_rate.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Omission Errors
text:
Overview
Omission errors are study list items that were not recalled. Analyzing omission patterns by serial position or other factors reveals which items are hardest to retrieve.

title: Omission Errors
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.omission_error_rate import omission_error_rate, plot_omission_error_rate

rate = omission_error_rate(data)
plot_omission_error_rate(datasets=[data], labels=["Data"])

title: Custom Analyses
text: jaxcmr analyses follow consistent patterns. You can create custom analyses that integrate with the library.

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Analysis Pattern
Most analyses follow this structure:
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jaxtyping import Array, Integer, Float

def my_analysis(
    recalls: Integer[Array, "trials max_recalls"],
    list_length: int,
    **kwargs
) -> Float[Array, "..."]:
    """
    Compute my custom analysis.

    Args:
        recalls: Recall sequences (1-indexed, 0 = padding)
        list_length: Number of items in each list
        **kwargs: Additional parameters

    Returns:
        Analysis results
    """
    # Your implementation here
    ...

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Example: First Recall Probability
Probability of recalling each serial position first:
def first_recall_probability(
    recalls: Integer[Array, "trials max_recalls"],
    list_length: int,
) -> Float[Array, "list_length"]:
    """Probability each position is recalled first."""
    # Get first recall for each trial
    first_recalls = recalls[:, 0]

    # Count how often each position is first
    counts = jnp.zeros(list_length)
    for pos in range(1, list_length + 1):
        counts = counts.at[pos - 1].set(jnp.sum(first_recalls == pos))

    # Normalize
    return counts / jnp.sum(counts)

title: Custom Analyses
text:
JIT Compatibility
For performance, ensure your analysis is JIT-compatible:
import jax

@jax.jit
def my_fast_analysis(recalls, list_length):
    ...
Avoid Python loops over data—use JAX operations instead.

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Vectorization
Use jax.vmap to apply analyses across subjects or conditions:
# Apply analysis to each subject separately
subject_results = jax.vmap(my_analysis)(subject_data)

Category SPC shows serial position curves broken down by item category.

from jaxcmr.analyses.cat_spc import plot_cat_spc

title: Concepts
text: This section covers the foundational concepts you need to understand jaxcmr.

title: Concepts
text:
What is jaxcmr?
jaxcmr is a JAX-accelerated toolkit for computational memory research. It provides:

Analyses: Functions for computing behavioral measures from recall data
Models: Implementations of CMR and related memory search models
Evaluation: Tools for fitting models to data and comparing them

title: Concepts
text:
Core Design
The library is built around typed protocols that define extension points. Implement these protocols and your code integrates with the entire ecosystem.

title: Concepts
text:
Sections

Why JAX - JAX fundamentals for memory science
Data Format - The RecallDataset structure
Architecture - How models are composed
Evaluation Pipeline - Fitting and simulation flow

title: Data Format
text: jaxcmr expects data in a specific format defined by the RecallDataset TypedDict.

title: Data Format
text:
RecallDataset Structure
class RecallDataset(TypedDict):
    # Required fields
    subject: Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]
    listLength: Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]
    pres_itemnos: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_presented"]
    recalls: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]

    # Optional fields
    pres_itemids: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_presented"]
    rec_itemids: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]]
    irt: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]]
    session: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]]

title: Data Format
text:
Field Descriptions

Required Fields








Field
Shape
Description




subject
(n_trials, 1)
Subject ID for each trial


listLength
(n_trials, 1)
Number of items presented in each trial


pres_itemnos
(n_trials, max_presented)
Within-list item positions (1-indexed, 0 = padding)


recalls
(n_trials, max_recalled)
Recalled item positions (1-indexed, 0 = padding)





Optional Fields








Field
Shape
Description




pres_itemids
(n_trials, max_presented)
Cross-list item IDs (for semantic analyses)


rec_itemids
(n_trials, max_recalled)
Cross-list IDs of recalled items


irt
(n_trials, max_recalled)
Inter-response times


session
(n_trials, 1)
Session identifiers

title: Data Format
text:
Indexing Convention
All item indices are 1-indexed: - Position 1 = first item in the list - Position 0 = padding (unused entry)

title: Data Format
text:
Loading Data
Data is typically stored in HDF5 format:
import h5py

with h5py.File("experiment.h5", "r") as f:
    dataset = {key: f[key][:] for key in f.keys()}

title: Data Format
text:
Converting from Other Formats
Coming soon: conversion utilities for psifr, pandas DataFrames

title: Get Started
text:
Install the latest stable version using pip:
pip install jaxcmr
Or install the development version from GitHub:
pip install git+https://github.com/githubpsyche/jaxcmr


jaxcmr uses JAX for fast computation. By default, JAX runs on CPU. For GPU acceleration:
# CUDA (NVIDIA GPUs)
pip install -U "jax[cuda12]"

# Metal (Apple Silicon)
pip install jax-metal

title: jaxcmr
text: jaxcmr is a Python library for specifying and evaluating computational models of memory search. It leverages a compositional design and JAX’s support for easy just-in-time compilation to streamline model prototyping and simulation so researchers can explore more ideas in less time.

title: jaxcmr
text:
Installation
pip install jaxcmr
Or install the development version:
pip install git+https://github.com/githubpsyche/jaxcmr

title: jaxcmr
text:
Explore


Get Started
Installation and quickstart tutorials.


Concepts
Library architecture, JAX fundamentals, and core abstractions.


Analyses
Behavioral measures for recall data.


Models
CMR and memory search model variants.


Evaluation
Fitting, simulation, and model comparison.


Workflow
Research practice, templates, and tooling.


Projects
Research manuscripts using jaxcmr.


Blog
Updates and research notes.


GitHub
Source code and issue tracker.

title: Architecture
text: jaxcmr models are composed from three core components, each defined by a protocol.

title: Architecture
text:
The MemorySearch Protocol
All memory search models implement the MemorySearch protocol:
class MemorySearch(Protocol):
    item_count: int
    is_active: Bool[Array, ""]
    recallable: Bool[Array, " item_count"]
    recall_total: Integer[Array, ""]

    def experience(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def start_retrieving(self) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def retrieve(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def activations(self) -> Float[Array, " item_count"]: ...
    def outcome_probability(self, choice: Int_) -> Float[Array, ""]: ...
    def outcome_probabilities(self) -> Float[Array, " recall_outcomes"]: ...
This defines the interface for: - Study phase: experience() processes each study item - Transition: start_retrieving() switches to retrieval mode - Retrieval phase: retrieve() processes each recall, activations() and outcome_probability() compute retrieval probabilities

title: Architecture
text:
Component Protocols
Models are built from three component types:

Context
Maintains temporal context that drifts during study and retrieval:
class Context(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    initial_state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    size: int

    def integrate(self, context_input, drift_rate) -> "Context": ...


Memory
Stores and retrieves associations:
class Memory(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " input_size output_size"]

    def associate(self, in_pattern, out_pattern, learning_rate) -> "Memory": ...
    def probe(self, in_pattern) -> Float[Array, " output_size"]: ...


TerminationPolicy
Determines when to stop recalling:
class TerminationPolicy(Protocol):
    def stop_probability(self, model: "MemorySearch") -> Float[Array, ""]: ...

title: Architecture
text:
Composition
A CMR model combines these components:
MemorySearch
├── Context (temporal context state)
├── Memory (MFC: item → context)
├── Memory (MCF: context → item)
└── TerminationPolicy (when to stop)

title: Architecture
text:
Factory Functions
Factory protocols enable dependency injection:
class ContextCreateFn(Protocol):
    def __call__(self, item_count: int) -> Context: ...

class MemoryCreateFn(Protocol):
    def __call__(self, list_length, parameters, context) -> Memory: ...
This allows swapping implementations without changing model code.

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text: Model evaluation connects loss functions, fitting algorithms, and simulation.

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text:
Pipeline Overview
Dataset → LossFnGenerator → Loss Function → FittingAlgorithm → FitResult
                                ↑
                          TrialSimulator

title: Evaluation Pipeline
Evaluation Pipeline
text:
FittingAlgorithm
Runs optimization:
class FittingAlgorithm(Protocol):
    def __init__(
        self,
        dataset: RecallDataset,
        features: Optional[...],
        base_params: Mapping[str, Float_],
        model_factory: Type[MemorySearchModelFactory],
        loss_fn_generator: Type[LossFnGenerator],
        hyperparams: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,
    ): ...

    def fit(
        self,
        trial_mask: Bool[Array, " trials"],
        fit_to_subjects: bool = True,
    ) -> FitResult: ...

title: Evaluation Pipeline
Evaluation Pipeline
text:
FitResult
Fitting returns a structured result:
class FitResult(TypedDict):
    fixed: dict[str, float]        # Fixed parameters
    free: dict[str, list[float]]   # Free parameter bounds
    fitness: list[float]           # Fitness values
    fits: dict[str, list[float]]   # Optimized parameter values
    hyperparameters: dict[str, Any]
    fit_time: float

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text:
Typical Workflow
# 1. Load data
dataset = load_dataset("experiment.h5")

# 2. Configure fitting
algorithm = ScipyDE(
    dataset=dataset,
    base_params=DEFAULT_PARAMS,
    model_factory=CMRFactory,
    loss_fn_generator=RecallLikelihood,
    hyperparams={"bounds": param_bounds}
)

# 3. Run fitting
result = algorithm.fit(trial_mask, fit_to_subjects=True)

# 4. Inspect results
print(result["fits"])
print(result["fitness"])

title: Why JAX
text: jaxcmr uses JAX as its computational backend. Understanding a few JAX concepts helps you get the most from the library.

title: Why JAX
text:
Why JAX for Memory Modeling?
Memory models require:

Fast simulation - Thousands of trials to evaluate a parameter set
Parallel fitting - Multiple subjects, multiple models
Reproducibility - Identical random seeds yield identical results

JAX addresses all three through its core features.

title: Why JAX
text:
Key JAX Concepts

JIT Compilation
jax.jit compiles Python functions to optimized machine code:
import jax

@jax.jit
def simulate_trial(model, study_items):
    # This runs much faster after first call
    ...
jaxcmr models are designed to be JIT-compatible. Most functions work seamlessly with jit.


Vectorization with vmap
jax.vmap automatically vectorizes functions across an axis:
# Instead of looping over trials:
for trial in trials:
    result = simulate(trial)

# Vectorize:
results = jax.vmap(simulate)(trials)
This enables parallel simulation across trials and subjects.


Pure Functions
JAX functions must be pure—no side effects, same inputs always produce same outputs. This enables:

Reproducibility: Random state is explicit (PRNGKey)
Parallelism: No hidden state to synchronize
Caching: Results can be safely cached



PyTrees
JAX represents nested data structures (dicts, dataclasses) as “PyTrees”. jaxcmr models are PyTrees, making them compatible with JAX transformations:
# Models can be passed through jit, vmap, etc.
model = CMR.create(list_length=16, parameters=params)
jax.tree_util.tree_map(lambda x: x.shape, model)  # Inspect structure

title: Why JAX
text:
Practical Implications
When working with jaxcmr:

Use JIT for fitting - Wrap loss functions in jit for speed
Vectorize over trials - Use vmap instead of loops
Manage random state explicitly - Pass PRNGKey to functions that sample
Models are immutable - Methods return new model instances

title: Why JAX
text:
Further Reading

JAX Quickstart
JAX Sharp Bits

title: Intrusion Errors
text:
Note



See templates/intrusion_error_rate.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Intrusion Errors
text:
Overview
Intrusion errors occur when participants recall items that were not on the study list. These can be:

Prior-list intrusions (items from previous lists)
Extra-experimental intrusions (items never studied)

title: Intrusion Errors
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.intrusion_error_rate import intrusion_error_rate, plot_intrusion_error_rate

rate = intrusion_error_rate(data)
plot_intrusion_error_rate(datasets=[data], labels=["Data"])

title: Importing Data
text: Before computing analyses, you need to load your data into the RecallDataset format.

title: Importing Data
text:
From HDF5
The most common format for jaxcmr data:
import h5py
import jax.numpy as jnp

def load_dataset(path: str) -> dict:
    """Load a RecallDataset from HDF5."""
    with h5py.File(path, "r") as f:
        return {key: jnp.array(f[key][:]) for key in f.keys()}

dataset = load_dataset("experiment.h5")

title: Importing Data
text:
From psifr
If you have data in psifr format:
Coming soon: psifr conversion utilities

title: Importing Data
text:
From Pandas
Converting from a DataFrame:
Coming soon: DataFrame conversion utilities

title: Importing Data
text:
Validating Data
Check your dataset has the required fields:
required = ["subject", "listLength", "pres_itemnos", "recalls"]
for field in required:
    assert field in dataset, f"Missing required field: {field}"
See Data Format for complete field specifications.

title: Compound Cueing
text:
Note



See templates/compound_cueing.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Compound Cueing
text:
Overview
Compound cueing analysis examines how multiple cues (e.g., context + just-recalled item) combine to support retrieval.

title: Compound Cueing
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.compound_cueing_crp import plot_compound_cueing_crp

plot_compound_cueing_crp(datasets=[data], labels=["Data"])

title: Scoring Data
text: Before computing detailed analyses, you often need basic metrics about recall performance.

title: Scoring Data
text:
Basic Metrics

Recall Count
Number of correct recalls per trial:
import jax.numpy as jnp

def recall_count(recalls):
    """Count non-zero (valid) recalls per trial."""
    return jnp.sum(recalls > 0, axis=1)

counts = recall_count(dataset["recalls"])


Recall Probability
Proportion of items recalled:
def recall_probability(recalls, list_length):
    """Compute recall probability per trial."""
    counts = jnp.sum(recalls > 0, axis=1)
    return counts / list_length.squeeze()

title: Scoring Data
text:
Filtering Trials
You may want to filter trials by subject, session, or other criteria:
# Trials for subject 1
mask = dataset["subject"].squeeze() == 1
subject_recalls = dataset["recalls"][mask]

title: Scoring Data
text:
Next Steps
With data loaded and validated, you can compute:

Serial Position Curve - Recall by presentation position
Conditional Response Probability - Temporal contiguity

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Note



See templates/spc.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Overview
The Serial Position Curve (SPC) plots recall probability as a function of study position. It typically shows:

Primacy effect: Enhanced recall for early items
Recency effect: Enhanced recall for recent items
Asymmetry: Recency is often stronger than primacy in immediate free recall

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.spc import spc, plot_spc

# Compute SPC from recalls array
curve = spc(data["recalls"], list_length=16)

# Plot comparing model and data
plot_spc(
    datasets=[simulation, data],
    labels=["Model", "Data"],
)

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Function Reference

spc(recalls, list_length, trial_mask=None)
Compute serial position curve.
Parameters:

recalls: Array of shape (n_trials, max_recalls) with 1-indexed recalls
list_length: Number of items in the study list
trial_mask: Optional boolean mask to filter trials

Returns:

Array of shape (list_length,) with recall probability at each position



plot_spc(datasets, trial_masks, labels, ...)
Plot one or more SPCs with error bars.

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Interpretation



Pattern
Interpretation




Strong recency
Recent items have high activation


Strong primacy
Extra rehearsal or primacy gradient


Flat curve
Random or ceiling performance


U-shape
Typical free recall pattern

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Note



See templates/crp.ipynb for a complete example.

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Overview
The Conditional Response Probability (CRP) curve shows the probability of recalling an item as a function of its lag (study position relative to the just-recalled item).
Key features:

Temporal contiguity: Items studied nearby tend to be recalled together
Forward asymmetry: Transitions to +1 lag are more likely than -1
Peak at lag +1 and -1

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.crp import crp, plot_crp

# Compute CRP
result = crp(data["recalls"], list_length=16)

# Plot comparing model and data
plot_crp(
    datasets=[simulation, data],
    labels=["Model", "Data"],
)

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Interpretation



Pattern
Interpretation




Sharp peak at ±1
Strong temporal contiguity


Forward asymmetry
Context drift creates forward bias


Flat curve
Random transitions

title: CMR
text: CMR (Context Maintenance and Retrieval) is the base model for memory search in jaxcmr.

title: CMR
text:
Overview
CMR explains free recall through three mechanisms:

Context drift: During study, each item updates the temporal context
Association: Items are associated with their encoding context
Reinstatement: During retrieval, recalling an item reinstates its context, cueing nearby items

title: CMR
text:
Architecture
A CMR model combines:

A Context representation
Two Memory stores (MFC and MCF)
A TerminationPolicy for stopping

title: CMR
text:
Parameters
Coming soon: parameter documentation

title: CMR
text:
Usage
#| eval: false
from jaxcmr.models import CMR

# Create a model for a 16-item list
model = CMR.create(list_length=16, parameters=params)

title: Memory
text: Memory components store associations between items and context. CMR uses two memory stores:

title: Memory
text:
The Memory Protocol
Any memory implementation must satisfy the Memory protocol:
class Memory(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " input_size output_size"]

    def associate(
        self,
        in_pattern: Float[Array, " input_size"],
        out_pattern: Float[Array, " output_size"],
        learning_rate: Float_,
    ) -> "Memory":
        """Return the updated memory after associating input and output patterns."""
        ...

    def probe(
        self,
        in_pattern: Float[Array, " input_size"],
    ) -> Float[Array, " output_size"]:
        """Return the output pattern associated with the input pattern."""
        ...

title: Memory
text:
Available Implementations
Coming soon: documentation of memory variants

title: Custom Models
text: jaxcmr is designed for extensibility. You can create custom model variants by implementing the core protocols.

title: Custom Models
text:
The MemorySearch Protocol
All models must satisfy the MemorySearch protocol:
class MemorySearch(Protocol):
    item_count: int
    is_active: Bool[Array, ""]
    recallable: Bool[Array, " item_count"]
    recall_total: Integer[Array, ""]

    def experience(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def start_retrieving(self) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def retrieve(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def activations(self) -> Float[Array, " item_count"]: ...
    def outcome_probability(self, choice: Int_) -> Float[Array, ""]: ...
    def outcome_probabilities(self) -> Float[Array, " recall_outcomes"]: ...

title: Custom Models
text:
Creating a Custom Model
Coming soon: step-by-step guide

title: Parameters
text: This page documents all parameters used in jaxcmr CMR models, their meanings, and typical value ranges.

title: Parameters
text:
Context Drift Parameters
These control how the temporal context representation evolves.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




encoding_drift_rate
How much context changes when studying each item. Higher values = faster drift, more temporal distinctiveness
0.3 - 0.9


start_drift_rate
Context drift at the transition from study to recall. Often similar to encoding drift rate
0.3 - 0.9


recall_drift_rate
Context drift after recalling each item. Controls how much recalled items reinstate their context
0.3 - 0.9



Intuition: With low drift rates, context changes slowly and distant items share similar contexts. With high drift rates, each item has a more distinct context, increasing temporal precision.

title: Parameters
text:
Primacy Parameters
These create the primacy effect (better memory for early list items).








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




primacy_scale
Multiplier for the first item’s learning rate. Higher = stronger primacy
1.0 - 5.0


primacy_decay
Exponential decay rate across serial positions. Higher = primacy effect decays faster
0.3 - 1.5



The learning rate for position \(i\) is:
\[\text{lr}_i = \text{primacy\_scale} \times e^{-\text{primacy\_decay} \times i}\]

title: Parameters
text:
Association Strength Parameters
These control the strength of item-context associations.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




learning_rate
Base strength for new MFC associations (item → context)
0.3 - 1.0


shared_support
Pre-experimental MCF association strength (all context units → all items)
0.01 - 0.2


item_support
Additional MCF association strength for matching context-item pairs
0.1 - 0.5



Intuition: shared_support creates a baseline retrieval probability for all items. item_support gives each item extra support from its corresponding context unit.

title: Parameters
text:
Retrieval Parameters
These control the retrieval process.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




choice_sensitivity
Softmax temperature for converting activations to probabilities. Higher = more deterministic (winner-take-all)
0.1 - 2.0



With choice_sensitivity = 0, retrieval is random among available items. As it increases, the most activated item becomes more likely to be retrieved.

title: Parameters
text:
Stopping Parameters
These control when recall terminates (using PositionalTermination).








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




stop_probability_scale
Base probability of stopping at first recall position
0.01 - 0.2


stop_probability_growth
Exponential growth rate of stopping probability
0.1 - 0.5



Stop probability at recall position \(i\) is:
\[P(\text{stop})_i = \text{scale} \times e^{\text{growth} \times i}\]

title: Parameters
text:
Boolean Parameters








Parameter
Description
Default




learn_after_context_update
If true, associations use the updated (post-drift) context state
Varies


allow_repeated_recalls
If true, items can be recalled multiple times
False

title: Parameters
text:
Example Parameter Set
A complete parameter dictionary for CMR:
params = {
    # Context drift
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,

    # Primacy
    "primacy_scale": 1.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.6,

    # Association strength
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,

    # Retrieval
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,

    # Stopping
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,

    # Boolean flags
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

title: Parameters
text:
Model Variants
Different CMR variants may require additional parameters:

Semantic CMR
For models with semantic similarity:







Parameter
Description




semantic_scale
Weight for semantic similarity in retrieval competition





Positional CMR
For models with positional encoding:







Parameter
Description




position_encoding_weight
Weight for positional vs. temporal context

title: Fitting
text:
Note



For the full workflow with all options, see the fitting template notebook.

title: Fitting
text:
Overview
Model fitting finds parameter values that best explain observed recall data using differential evolution optimization.

title: Fitting
text:
1. Load Your Data
from jaxcmr.helpers import load_data, generate_trial_mask

# Load dataset
data = load_data("path/to/your/data.h5")

# Optional: filter trials (e.g., by list type)
trial_mask = generate_trial_mask(data, "data['listtype'] == 1")

title: Fitting
text:
2. Define Parameters
Specify which parameters to fit and their bounds:
parameters = {
    "fixed": {
        "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
        "learn_after_context_update": False,
    },
    "free": {
        "encoding_drift_rate": [0.0, 1.0],
        "start_drift_rate": [0.0, 1.0],
        "recall_drift_rate": [0.0, 1.0],
        "shared_support": [0.0, 100.0],
        "item_support": [0.0, 100.0],
        "learning_rate": [0.0, 1.0],
        "primacy_scale": [0.0, 100.0],
        "primacy_decay": [0.0, 100.0],
        "choice_sensitivity": [0.0, 100.0],
        "stop_probability_scale": [0.0, 1.0],
        "stop_probability_growth": [0.0, 1.0],
    },
}

title: Fitting
text:
3. Configure the Model
from jaxcmr.models.cmr import make_factory
from jaxcmr.components.linear_memory import init_mfc, init_mcf
from jaxcmr.components.context import init as init_context
from jaxcmr.components.termination import PositionalTermination

# Create model factory
model_factory = make_factory(
    mfc_create_fn=init_mfc,
    mcf_create_fn=init_mcf,
    context_create_fn=init_context,
    termination_policy_create_fn=PositionalTermination,
)

title: Fitting
text:
4. Set Up the Fitter
from jaxcmr.fitting import ScipyDE
from jaxcmr.loss.sequence_likelihood import SequenceLikelihoodFnGenerator

fitter = ScipyDE(
    data,
    features=None,  # or semantic embeddings
    base_params=parameters["fixed"],
    model_factory=model_factory,
    loss_fn_generator=SequenceLikelihoodFnGenerator,
    hyperparams={
        "bounds": parameters["free"],
        "num_steps": 1000,
        "pop_size": 15,
        "best_of": 3,  # restart optimization 3 times
        "progress_bar": True,
    },
)

title: Fitting
text:
5. Run the Fit
# Fit all subjects
results = fitter.fit(trial_mask)

# Results contain fitted parameters for each subject
print(results["fits"])  # dict: parameter_name -> [subject1_val, subject2_val, ...]
print(results["fitness"])  # list of fitness values per subject

title: Fitting
text:
6. Examine Results
from jaxcmr.summarize import summarize_parameters

# Get summary statistics
query_params = list(parameters["free"].keys())
summary = summarize_parameters(
    [results],
    query_params,
    include_std=True,
    include_ci=True,
)
print(summary)

title: Fitting
text:
7. Simulate from Fitted Parameters
from jaxcmr.simulation import simulate_h5_from_h5
from jax import random
import jax.numpy as jnp

rng = random.PRNGKey(0)
fitted_params = {k: jnp.array(v) for k, v in results["fits"].items()}

sim = simulate_h5_from_h5(
    model_factory,
    data,
    features=None,
    params=fitted_params,
    trial_mask=trial_mask,
    experiment_count=100,  # simulations per subject
    rng=rng,
)

title: Fitting
text:
8. Compare Model to Data
from jaxcmr.analyses.spc import plot_spc

# Compare serial position curves
plot_spc(
    datasets=[sim, data],
    trial_masks=[trial_mask, trial_mask],
    labels=["Model", "Data"],
)

title: Fitting
text:
Loss Functions







Loss Function
Use Case




SequenceLikelihoodFnGenerator
Standard recall sequence likelihood


ExcludeTerminationLikelihoodFnGenerator
Likelihood ignoring stop decisions


SPCMSEFnGenerator
Fit to serial position curve shape

title: Fitting
text:
Fitting Algorithm Options
Currently jaxcmr provides ScipyDE (differential evolution). Key hyperparameters:



Parameter
Description
Typical Value




num_steps
Max optimizer iterations
500-2000


pop_size
Population size
10-20


best_of
Number of restarts
1-5


relative_tolerance
Convergence threshold
0.001

title: Fitting
text:
The FittingAlgorithm Protocol
Custom fitting algorithms should implement:
class FittingAlgorithm(Protocol):
    def __init__(
        self,
        dataset: RecallDataset,
        features: Optional[...],
        base_params: Mapping[str, Float_],
        model_factory: Type[MemorySearchModelFactory],
        loss_fn_generator: Type[LossFnGenerator],
        hyperparams: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,
    ): ...

    def fit(
        self,
        trial_mask: Bool[Array, " trials"],
        fit_to_subjects: bool = True,
    ) -> FitResult: ...

title: Simulation
text: Simulation generates recall sequences from a model given study lists.

title: Simulation
text:
The TrialSimulator Protocol
class TrialSimulator(Protocol):
    def __call__(
        self,
        model: MemorySearch,
        present: Integer[Array, " study_events"],
        trial: Integer[Array, " recalls"],
        rng: PRNGKeyArray,
    ) -> tuple[MemorySearch, Integer[Array, " recall_events"]]:
        """Returns model and simulated recall sequence."""
        ...

title: Projects
text:
Research manuscripts and projects using jaxcmr for computational memory modeling.













TalmiEEG


Investigating EEG correlates of memory encoding and retrieval using CMR models. Explores how neural signals relate to model parameters.


Status: In progress




RepFR


Repetition effects in free recall. Modeling how repeated items affect temporal context and retrieval dynamics.


Status: In progress




CRU to CMR


Comparing Context Retrieval and Updating (CRU) models with CMR. Systematic evaluation of model architectures and parameter recovery.


Status: Published




No matching items

title: Welcome to jaxcmr
text: Welcome to the jaxcmr blog! This is where we’ll share updates on the library, tutorials for computational memory modeling, and notes from ongoing research.

title: Welcome to jaxcmr
text:
What is jaxcmr?
jaxcmr is a JAX-accelerated toolkit for modeling memory search using Context Maintenance and Retrieval (CMR) models. It provides:

Fast simulation: JIT-compiled model simulations
Differentiable: JAX enables automatic differentiation
Fitting: Differential evolution for parameter estimation
Analyses: Standard behavioral measures (SPC, CRP, etc.)

title: Welcome to jaxcmr
text:
Getting Started
Check out the Getting Started guide to install jaxcmr and run your first simulation.

title: Welcome to jaxcmr
text:
Stay Updated
We’ll post here about:

New features and releases
Tutorials and tips
Research findings using jaxcmr
Technical deep-dives into CMR

Subscribe to the RSS feed to stay updated.
